other_observers: There is a long list of people who saw this bird after it was first identified by me. Most saw the bird on the following Saturday, the 29th of a January.; Brandon Percival first found this bird around 10 January, but it remained unidentified until I had a chance to study the bird in detail and obtain multiple photographs of it.   I was the first to identify the bird as Long-billed Thrasher.
specific_location: Chico Basin Ranch Rocky Mountain Bird Observatory Banding Station
description: This was a large brown-backed thrasher with long brown tail and cap. The cheeks were noticeable medium gray and the gray extended down onto the sides of its neck. The bill was blackish and slightly decurved. Breast, belly and undertail coverts were streaked with light black on a cream background color. Legs were dark. Eye was orange to yellow depending upon the light. The tail was very worn from feeding on the ground throughout the time it was at the Chico Basin Ranch.Behaviors: Some days this bird was a skulker prefering the densest matted Russian thistle piles but on other days it would forage on the open, always eating Russian olive fruits, sometimes feeding from fruits on the ground while at other times feeding from fruits still on the trees. On some days the bird would perch partial in the open and feed in various parts of the olive trees. It would often fly from the ground to put a tree brach between itself and me.Call: on one of my many visits I heard it sing its soft song for one phrase. It frequently gave a shark chakk call and a few times when I got too close it gave an alarm call, the rattle call described in SibleyPlumage: basic
